annotations.label: Arc length as integral of speed

anchor.snippet: the arc length is obtained by integrating speed

annotations.note: pathELength is defined as ∫⁻ t in Icc a b, ‖mfderiv γ t 1‖ₑ, i.e. the length as the integral of the norm of the velocity (speed), generalizing the Euclidean case to manifolds.

anchors.snippet: the arc length is obtained by integrating speed

annotations.label: Arc length formula for differentiable curve

anchor.snippet: The length of the curve is given by the formula

annotations.note: The integral formula L = ∫‖γ'‖ for a differentiable curve is exactly pathELength, of which the Euclidean √(Σ(dxᵢ/dt)²) form is a special case.

anchors.snippet: The length of the curve is given by the formula

annotations.label: Independence of parameterization

anchor.snippet: is the same regardless of the parameterization used to define the curve

annotations.note: pathELength_comp_of_monotoneOn proves the path length is invariant under monotone reparametrization.

annotations.label: Chordal distance

anchor.snippet: that approximation is known as the (cumulative) chordal distance

annotations.note: The cumulative chord sum ∑ edist(f(uᵢ₊₁), f(uᵢ)) appears inside the definition of eVariationOn but is not given its own named definition.

annotations.label: Refinement does not decrease chordal distance

anchor.snippet: the chordal distance will not decrease

annotations.note: eVariationOn.add_point shows that inserting a point into a partition does not decrease the chord sum, the single-step case from which general refinement monotonicity follows.

annotations.label: Length as limit of polygonal chains

anchor.snippet: this bound is the length of the curve

annotations.note: eVariationOn is defined as the supremum over partitions of the chord sums, which is exactly the definition of length as the limit/supremum of polygonal approximations, in general metric spaces.

annotations.label: Limit length equals integral length

anchor.snippet: this limiting length always equals the length obtained from integration

annotations.note: Both the supremum/polygonal definition (eVariationOn) and the integral definition (pathELength) exist, but no theorem equating them for differentiable curves was found.

annotations.label: Rectifiable curve

anchor.snippet: is called a rectifiable curve

annotations.note: A rectifiable curve is one of finite length, exactly BoundedVariationOn (eVariationOn f s ≠ ∞).

annotations.label: Signed arc length

anchor.snippet: A signed arc length can be defined to convey a sense of orientation

annotations.note: variationOnFromTo f s a b is the signed variation, negated when b < a, capturing the orientation-aware signed arc length.

annotations.label: Arc length for explicit function y=f(x)

anchor.snippet: then it is simply a special case of a parametric equation

annotations.note: No lemma giving L = ∫√(1+f'(x)²) for the graph of an explicit function was found, though it follows from pathELength.

anchors.snippet: then it is simply a special case of a parametric equation

annotations.note: No catenary/cycloid/cardioid arc-length computations were found in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no surface theory or first fundamental form formulation of the arc-length integrand.

annotations.note: Real.pi exists but is defined as twice a zero of cos in [1,2], not via the circumference-to-diameter ratio.

annotations.note: No theorem computing the arc length of a semicircle as πr was found.

anchor.snippet: This is a definition of the radian.

annotations.label: Arc length with angle in degrees

anchor.snippet: is in degrees

annotations.note: No degree-based circular arc-length formula was found in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: is in grads (100 grads, or grades, or gradians are one right-angle )

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of grads or a grad-based arc-length formula.

anchor.snippet: is in turns (one turn is a complete rotation

anchor.snippet: applies in the following circumstances

annotations.note: No great-circle distance / geographic arc-length computation was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curves of infinite length

anchor.snippet: such curves are said to have infinite length

annotations.note: Infinite length is representable since eVariationOn is ℝ≥0∞-valued (value ∞, i.e. the negation of BoundedVariationOn), but there is no dedicated 'infinite-length curve' definition.

annotations.label: Koch curve

anchor.snippet: An example of such a curve is the Koch curve.

annotations.note: The Koch curve is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph of x sin(1/x)

anchor.snippet: Another example of a curve with infinite length is the graph of the function defined by

annotations.note: No example showing the graph of x·sin(1/x) has infinite length / unbounded variation was found.

annotations.label: Length of a curve on a (pseudo-)Riemannian manifold

anchor.snippet: is defined to be

annotations.note: pathELength gives the length of a path on a (Riemannian/normed-tangent) manifold as ∫‖γ'‖, but the pseudo-Riemannian/Lorentzian case (where g(v,v) may be negative) is not covered.

annotations.label: Curve length is non-negative

anchor.snippet: Thus the length of a curve is a non-negative real number.

annotations.note: pathELength (and eVariationOn) are valued in ℝ≥0∞, so length is non-negative by construction.

annotations.label: Arc length as proper time/distance in relativity

anchor.snippet: arc length of timelike curves ( world lines ) is the proper time elapsed along the world line

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Lorentzian/pseudo-Riemannian geometry, so proper time along world lines is not formalized.
